title: Bill to Abolish Abortion in South Carolina Filed by State Rep. Jonathan Hill
author: James Silberman
orientation: Free the States announces that on March 9, 2021, South Carolina State Rep. Jonathan Hill filed H4046, the South Carolina Unborn Victims of Violence Act of 2021, which would totally and immediately abolish abortion in the state by classifying it as murder under existing homicide statute. The article highlights two novel enforcement provisions targeting state officials and judges who collaborate with federal abortion rulings.
audiences: pro-life advocate evaluating incrementalist vs. abolitionist strategies
constitutional law observer interested in nullification and federalism

questions.question: What bill was introduced and what would it actually do in South Carolina?
questions.answer: H4046, the South Carolina Unborn Victims of Violence Act of 2021, would totally and immediately abolish abortion in South Carolina by treating any deliberate termination of an unborn child as murder under the state's existing homicide statute (Section 16-3-10), giving preborn children the same protection as anyone else.
questions.quote: H4046 establishes that “The deliberate termination of an unborn child by any means or at any stage of development is murder under Section 16-3-10.” Abortion would be homicide and the protection afforded to our right to life would be extended equally to children in the womb.

questions.question: When did human life begin according to the bill's findings?
questions.audience: pro-life advocate evaluating incrementalist vs. abolitionist strategies
questions.answer: The bill stakes its entire premise on the finding that personhood and the right to life begin at conception, which is what justifies treating abortion at any stage as homicide rather than as a regulated medical procedure.
questions.quote: (1)    The life of an individual human being begins at conception.

questions.question: How does the bill justify defying federal court rulings like Roe v. Wade?
questions.audience: constitutional law observer interested in nullification and federalism
questions.answer: The bill argues that the U.S. Constitution grants no right to abortion, that the Tenth Amendment reserves such powers to the states, and that any federal judicial ruling purporting to create such a right is not 'in pursuance of' the Constitution and therefore is not the supreme law of the land and not binding on South Carolina.
questions.quote: (11)    Any action of the federal judiciary that purports to provide a right to kill an unborn child is not made in pursuance of the Constitution of the United States, is not the supreme law of the land, and is not binding upon this State.

questions.question: How does the bill characterize the moral magnitude of legal abortion in America?
questions.answer: The bill explicitly labels nearly five decades of legal abortion as genocide, and uses that framing to argue that no judicial body has legitimate authority to authorize it.
questions.quote: (10)    Taking the life of more than sixty-two million unborn children over the last forty-eight years and counting constitutes genocide, and authorizing genocide is not within the legitimate powers of the federal judiciary.

questions.question: What's actually new about Hill's bill compared to other abolition bills filed in other states?
questions.answer: Beyond the standard defiance language, H4046 adds two enforcement teeth: it makes it a misdemeanor for any South Carolina official or law enforcement officer to cooperate with federal efforts that violate the right to life, and it makes any state judge who violates that right subject to impeachment under Article XV of the South Carolina Constitution.
questions.quote:
1. Any South Carolina official or law enforcement officer who cooperates with the federal government to violate the right to life is guilty of a misdemeanor, and
2. Any South Carolina judge who violates the right to life of preborn children shall be subject to impeachment in accordance with Article XV of the South Carolina Constitution.

questions.question: Where does the bill stand procedurally and what action does the article ask readers to take?
questions.answer: H4046 has been assigned to the South Carolina House Judiciary Committee under Chair Chris Murphy. Free the States is asking constituents to call Murphy at (803) 734-3120 to demand a hearing and a 'Yes' vote on the bill.
questions.quote: The bill has been assigned to the South Carolina House Judiciary Committee chaired by State Rep. Chris Murphy. Call Murphy at (803) 734-3120 to request a hearing and a ‘Yes’ vote for H4046.

questions.question: Why does the bill invoke legislators' oaths of office as a basis for defying federal rulings?
questions.answer: The bill grounds its defiance in the personal oath every General Assembly member swears, framing the protection of unborn life as a sworn duty to defend both the state and federal constitutions, not merely a policy preference.
questions.quote: (4)    The members of the General Assembly have solemnly sworn to the best of their abilities to “discharge the duties of our office and preserve, protect, and defend the Constitution of this State and of the United States. So help us God.”

questions.question: Does the bill claim states can ignore the federal judiciary anytime they disagree with it?
questions.audience: constitutional law observer interested in nullification and federalism
questions.answer: No. The bill explicitly cautions against reflexive nullification, conceding that prudence requires restraint, but argues that the federal judiciary is fallible and limited, leaving room for states to reject rulings that fall outside the Constitution's actual text.
questions.quote: (7)    Though prudence dictates that states should not declare actions of the federal judiciary void for light or indefinite causes, the federal judiciary is not infallible nor are its powers unlimited.

questions.question: Is this the first time South Carolina has considered abolishing abortion outright?
questions.answer: Yes. According to Free the States, this is the first abolition bill ever filed in South Carolina, marking the state's entry into the growing list of states with active equal-protection abolition legislation.
questions.quote: For the first time, South Carolina has a bill to abolish abortion!

related.title: Abortion Abolition Bill Introduced in Washington
related.reason: Earlier example of a state-level abolition bill being filed, providing context for the multi-state strategy that South Carolina's H4046 joins.

related.title: Alaska State Reps. Introduce Abortion Abolition Bill
related.reason: Another peer abolition bill in a different state, useful for comparing how each state's findings and enforcement provisions differ from H4046.

related.title: Missouri Abolition Bill Author Mike Moon Elected to State Senate
related.reason: Profiles a fellow legislator championing abolition, parallel to Jonathan Hill's role in South Carolina.

related.title: Alaska Abolition Bill Author David Eastman Wins Primary
related.reason: Shows electoral durability of legislators who file abolition bills, relevant to the political viability of Hill's effort.

related.title: State Rep. Bryan Slaton Refiles Texas Abolition Bill in Special Session
related.reason: A near-contemporaneous abolition bill in Texas, useful for tracking the broader 2021 wave of equal-protection legislation that H4046 was part of.

related.title: South Carolina Baptist Convention Passes Resolution Calling For Abolition
related.reason: Direct South Carolina follow-up showing growing institutional support for the abolition position Hill's bill embodies.
